title: [Flareon] Battle Guide
summary: A [Fire]-type physical breaker that runs [guts] and [facade] to weaponize burns into raw damage, backed by 130 Attack and 110 Special Defense.

bodyMarkdown:
## Overview
[Flareon] is a [sing]le-type [Fire] physical breaker with an unusual stat distribution. The role it fills is a [guts] and [facade] breaker that turns oppo[sing] burn attempts into damage boosts. [Flareon] isn't a sweeper — its job is to come in on a [sing]le predicted turn and unload a decisive [flare-blitz] or [facade] into whichever Pokemon the team needs removed.

## Strengths
The 130 Attack stat is the highest raw attack among [Fire]-types of its era, and with [guts] active it reaches an effective 195. [facade] is the defining move: when [Flareon] is burned, [guts] negates the burn's Attack drop and [facade] doubles in power, producing one of the hardest [sing]le-hit moves in the game. 110 Special Defense is also an underappreciated stat that lets [Flareon] [absorb] a stray special hit when needed.

## Weaknesses & Answers
The most limiting problem is Speed. 65 is slow enough that [Flareon] almost always moves second, and any faster attacker with a [Water], [Ground], or [Rock] move [revenge]-kills it. The cove[rage] pool is narrow for a pure [Fire]. [Flareon] also struggles against bulky [Water]s that don't care about the [Fire] STAB and can two-shot it with [scald] or [hydro-pump].

## Recommended Moveset
[flare-blitz] is the locked [Fire] STAB for maximum damage output, though the re[coil] does stack up over long matches. [facade] is the [guts] payoff move. [superpower] is the cove[rage] slot that handles [Rock], [Dark], and [Steel] walls that otherwise resist the STAB. The fourth slot is [quick-attack] as a priority [revenge] tool to compensate for the Speed issue.

## Item · Ability · Tera
[charcoal] is the clean-damage pick that boosts [Fire] STAB with no drawbacks, fitting a four-move set nicely. [focus-sash] is an alternate option for a lead-like role, ensuring [Flareon] survives long enough to use one big attack. The ability must be [guts] in the format. Tera [Fire] maxes [flare-blitz] damage, while Tera [Normal] specifically amplifies [facade] for the burn-abuse build.

## Teammates & Synergy
[Flareon] needs Speed control to function. A [Ground] partner answers the [Rock], [Water], and [Electric] threats that would otherwise dominate [Flareon], and a [stealth-rock] user chips oppo[sing] switches so [Flareon]'s one big attack finishes off the target. A status remover helps prevent the opponent from removing the burn before [Flareon] uses it.

## Play Patterns
[Flareon] is a breaker, not a cleaner. With flame-orb the plan is to come in, take the burn self-applied, and hit [facade] for massive damage; with [charcoal] the plan is raw [flare-blitz] plus [superpower] depending on the matchup. Keep [Flareon] in the back of the order so it isn't chipped by hazards or priority before its entry turn.
